speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to stop for a minute, or no?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. I'm ready. You're fine, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You OK? You ready to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three; July 9th, 1995; interview with Maurice Myers, in Flushing, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Standby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Mr. Myers, what happened after that first round up of Jews in Ostrowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the-- the round-up, as I told you, it was a smaller-- a few streets, only one or two streets. They didn't have enough Jews anymore. And these Jews came-- came to the steel mill, working. And at night, they went back to these-- to these couple of streets. This was-- this was going on like this till-- until December was the next [GERMAN]. Then they had the-- the second one, the second [GERMAN]-- the leftovers. Then with the left overs, already, they took my father, and mother, and-- and one of my sisters. Only the youngest one survived. How they came in the steel mill, I got acquainted with a Polack, a young Polack, which he was doing business. He was selling documents, Polish documents. He was selling-- doing, you know, errands for Jews, for money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I-- I remember I-- I paid him something like 200 zlotys or something for-- he brought me a birth certificate of Stefan Grabowski, with the same birth, with everything. And I had-- I-- I made a kencarte already out of it, with the pictures, with the whole thing. And I had Polish papers, which I-- why did I do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was sitting in the office, when my boys were working, till 5 o'clock, you know, from-- from 8:00 to 5:00-- and I was sometimes talking to this engineer. As I told you, he was a friend of ours too, because his wife, and he was our customers in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, tell me. You have in your department 3-- 30 boys of mine. Do they bring you any help? Do-- do you need them?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me, Mr Myers, not only I don't need them, but they are ruining my workers. They are spoiling my workers. If it would be up to me, I wouldn't keep one Jew. Because it were-- the-- the-- the things which one guy has to do, four guys are doing this job. But one Polack can do it, and he sees that. But the German-- Nazis ask me to keep them, and I keep them. So in every department, we have about 1,500 of you here. We don't need you here. But they ask us to keep you, and we keeping you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I say, uh-oh. This is also no good. This is-- this is not gonna be good. They keep us for the time being, and later, we will go. They'll go, time for us. I was always pessimistic. Now, who is Stefan Grabowski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stefan-- Stefan Grabowski, he told me this [NON-ENGLISH]-- told me he died, and I have his papers. So it is a real guy, and this was a real birth certificate. I had to believe him-- maybe yes, maybe no. Who knows? I couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Where do I have the money? That's a good question. When the war started in 1939, or let's say later, when-- when the ghetto started already, I was the only son; and my daughter, with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we didn't have money in the bank. There was no bank accounts. But a few $20-- gold $20 bills, $20 coins, which a $20 coin was worth at like $200. And my father still had a few hundred dollars, whatever he could save up all his life. And then my mother had a pair of diamond rings, and a ring, diamond earrings, and a ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and I had a-- a little box, you know. This was my whole thing. I put in the shoes, in the heels. I put on-- a 20-- a $20 coin in one heel, a $20 coin in the other heel. I took my mother's gold pieces and made a buckle, which I have till today. I forgot to bring it to show it to you. It went in-- a lot of gold in it-- a heavy buckle. But I made it black-- it shouldn't be shiny. And I was wearing it, just like I wear this-- I was wearing it. I had here in a-- in a uniform, I had a shirt, with also, pieces of gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had these pieces of gold, and I had all these things. It was in my possession. I was hiding it. Not sure when-- I didn't have anybody. My father, mother was gone. Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me to hide it, and I hide it. And this, thanks to this, it helped me to survive. But this is not the end of the picture. This-- this was the beginning of the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you didn't know what had happened your parents, you only knew they went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew they went to-- they went to Sandomierz. They went there a couple of weeks. And from Sandomierz, they went to Auschwitz, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time, though, did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We heard. We heard. It was-- we heard it. There were some witnesses who told us what-- they-- they were staying. They-- they took them to a-- to a other town, which they put together from more Jews. They should have a shipment, you know, they should have a shipment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you took on this new identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stefan Grabowski. Now, you left the steel mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still at the steel mill. And I was still-- it was December. I was still at the steel mill, and I talked to a boyfriend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Listen. You want to take over my job? I'm leaving. He said, are you kidding? Why are you leaving? You're not-- you have an office job. I say, you want my job? I'll give it to you. You are-- you-- you have the 29 boys to work with. I am leaving. You want to leave? You can do what you want. I am leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And naturally, I start growing this mustache, and I bought a hat. I should look like a peasant. You have to prepare yourself to it. And one day, this guy, this [NON-ENGLISH] who sold me these Polish papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you member his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. The other-- the other name, I can't remember. But it's [NON-ENGLISH]. Maybe it'll come to me. I can't remember the other name. It is such a long time. And he said, I have for you an apartment-- a room-- in Borso, in the big town. Nobody knows you there. There are trucks going. Because we couldn't use the railroad. We couldn't go into the railroad to the-- to the-- with the-- with the rails. A Jew cannot go in. They wouldn't sell you a ticket. And even if you have a ticket, they would recognize you-- forget it. And-- and we were hiding also from-- the Polacks shouldn't recognize you. Anyway, one day, he says, tomorrow, a truck is going to Warsaw-- a whole night, from 10 o'clock till the morning. And naturally, we paid for the truck, whatever the fare was. He took us, because he had to go to Warsaw from our town, for some reason. And he let us go in-- in the-- on the borders of Warsaw. He let us out from the truck, because he had to go to some other place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took, a doroshka. There were no taxi. You know doroshka, like they have in Central Park West, with the horses? Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the communication in town, in our town, because we didn't have cars yet. You know, there-- there was one car, the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] had the car. There was no cars in our town-- and-- and before the war. And we took, in Warsaw, a doroshka. And he told us he wants to go on this address. And we are sitting in the doroshka, this boy and me. And he told him the address. So he is going. He is going, going, going, and he stops right at the police station.

speaker: interviewer
text: He asked him, what are you doing? I told you which street you have to go. Yes, but I thought this other guy near you is Jewish, I thought. Here is the police station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Polish police, they had a Polish police, a supervisor-- a gestapo. But they wore the-- they wore the navy uniforms, the Polish police, with the round hats. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the first-- the first hello in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We went down. We had to go onto this Kommissariat. So, the guy-- you have money? You have money on you? How much money do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that I am-- I am not supposed to have money on me. I knew that. Because the money was-- somebody else already had the money in Warsaw, but another section.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said-- I had 50 zlotys I had. That's all you have? That's all I have, 50 zlotys. He took the 50 zlotys. So then they suddenly came in this German gestapo. What's going on? What is this guy doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he came with the doroshka, and he doesn't want to pay the fare. And he's here. The doroshkas took him in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you go with a doroshka, you have to pay, you-- you hear that? And he left to his office. And he took the $50, and he let me go-- the Polish-- just I want to show you-- a Polish doroshka.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were afraid for the Polacks more than of the German. A German wouldn't recognize me, but a Polack would. This was the reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you had a new identity now, and you were living in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I had an identity. I got a room. I got a room in-- in-- a nice room. This was-- her husband was a prisoner of war. The rate was like 3,000 zlotys. 3,000 zlotys was like $10 a month with food-- food. You-- you get breakfast, whatever food-- minimum food. No, I think without food-- we had to buy our own food, I think, here. But we had to pay the 3,000 zlotys. Because her rent was about 1,500 zlotys, and her husband was a prisoner of war. And she was with a daughter who didn't have to pay. So she kept a Jew. And it wouldn't take long. We were there about-- when was it-- January, February, 10--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, she didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- she was-- she knew, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you weren't use-- you were using a Polish identity, weren't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wouldn't-- I wouldn't-- I used the identity for people what didn't know, but she knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she was willing--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who would give her 3,000 zlotys. Maybe the whole apartment was worth 1,500 zlotys-- 1,200 she paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were taking a chance by her knowing that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but he recommended. This was a friend of his.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He recommended, and she agreed. If she wouldn't have the Jew, she wouldn't have the money. So she needed to pay the rent. She needed to buy food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where did you get money from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still had my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you had the savings.

speaker: interviewee
text: --my bundle-- my bundle. If I changed a $20-- a $20 coin, I had a fortune. I had a fortune of money. It was a lot of money. And this kept me alive. But not long I was in this apartment-- came February 10, 1943-- excuse me-- yes, 1943. February 10, a knock at the door-- a-- a big knock. And big knock-- I see, this is it. You know, when you heard the knock at the door, this is gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran to the kitchen. I didn't know where to hide. Between the dishes, she had-- she had a-- thing which she keep the dishes there, and-- and the pots and pans. So I pulled out some pots and pans, and I-- on the floor, I moved in. And I locked the-- closed the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And naturally, it was the real thing. Gestapo was in the house looking for me. Now, I don't know if this [NON-ENGLISH] send them-- it's possible-- or if she cooperate. But I don't believe so. Finally, they found me. Now, I had my coat, my winter coat hanging in the corridor with my hat. She didn't have a husband, so probably, they saw this hat. Because it took some time till they found me. It took maybe 15 minutes, they were in. I thought they left already, and they found-- they wanted to leave, but they saw a coat. And this is what the woman told me later. Though there must be a Jew in the house. And she probably show, with a finger or whatever. They opened. Naturally, bang right away in my-- in my face, that I thought I'm gonna die right away. Two boys, maybe 22-year-old-- two boys, 6 and 1/2, 7-feeters-- and with the Gestapo, with the Totenkopf, you know-- I never was near-- you wouldn't dare to stay near a gestapo Totenkopf. And so I was-- I saw that this is the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told them, oh you are the real gestapo Totenkopf. Then I said-- I-- I figured, in a couple of hours I'll be dead, right? In a couple of hours-- how long are they gonna let me live? I asked him, could you-- you know, I have some gold money buried in my town. Would you take me to my town, to the ghetto in my town, to the steel mill, and I will give you the gold which I buried there? He says, you there, [GERMAN], He says to me, what's the difference where you are gonna be dead? You have to be dead in Ostrog? Yes, you'll be dead right here. Why do you need to go so far? But this, what I said, the money-- he liked-- he liked the idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me from there right away to Aleja Szucha. Aleja Szucha was the nicest building what Poland has for the Secretary of State in Poland-- for this, the building they built before the war. And the gestapo took it over as their headquarters for Poland, for all Poland-- Aleja Szucha.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they took me into one of the offices. They gave me to one of their-- interrogate me. The first interrogation is-- was like this. Where did you see the underground paper? I say, I am myself underground. I don't have any paper. Lie down on the bench! And he had this leather-- what do you called it? This-- A whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: A whip-- a leather whip. And he gave me a one, on my behind. I thought, I'm gonna die for one. I got up, and I grabbed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, you have a gun here! You can shoot me! Why don't you shoot me? I'm not along-- and I start to yell. The doors for open from the officers. And a few officers start to come in. What's going on here? Why is there yelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, they [NON-ENGLISH]. This was it. He didn't hit me anymore. It was a good act. He didn't hit me anymore. They took me from there-- yes. OK. They-- they took me from there to the Pawiak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, are you figuring they haven't killed you because they think that you have information?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have information. It should get out the information on the underground. They had-- there was a Polish underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right

speaker: interviewee
text: But I--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew nothing of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I was the Polish underground. [LAUGHTER] I was myself an underground. I-- I was saving my life in underground. But this I wanted to-- to-- to tell you. I had-- I had-- how I saved money. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, the idea was, I put in here money, in the handkerchief, you see? And on my both hands--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --this is interesting. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] On my both hands, I had the amount like-- in one hand I had about a Polish $500-- 500 zloty pieces; like $200 in the other hand, about the same, or 50 more. And this-- it was winter time. I was wearing the long sleeve shirts, like this. On top of it I was wearing a sweater, and a jacket, and a coat. And when the Gestapo got me, I have to pick up my hand to hold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they bothered me, if I have some-- everything I had to put out on the table, my watch I have to take out, whatever money I had. But-- and-- and they checked me, but not here. Here, nobody's checking, you know what I mean? So I came to Pawiak, to the prison, with the money, and with the same story, over there, again-- hand to hold on the wall-- hand hold-- if I have any gun, a-- a knife, any-- anything on me, if I'm clean. I was clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: They take me to a small room to undress, because I have to get a shower. And my clothing goes into the steam, to-- and lousing. You know, there shouldn't be anything on them. But the shoes, the leather shoes, they don't take, because they cannot take.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, an Ukrainian stays on me, behind me, and undress everything. I take off my coat, and I take off my jacket. I'm already in my shirt. What do I do with this? So what do I do with it? He stays on me, and he sees what I'm doing. So I'm taking off this, and I took with the handkerchief, with the money, and I gave it to him-- one hand, I gave it to him. It was about-- like, worth $200-- Polish zlotys.

speaker: interviewee
text: He saw this-- so much money. He ran to the office, to his officer-- to his-- his officer was Oberste Fuhrer Miller, a German gestapo man. He was responsible for the-- for the whole floor. On every floor was another officer responsible. Every 24 hours, they changed. 24 hours was Oberste Fuhrer Miller, the next 24 hours, Oberste Fuhrer [NON-ENGLISH]. These two, every 24 hours. He took the money. I took the other handkerchief. I put them in my shoes with the socks. I covered them. And I went to the bath to have a shower. I soak myself-- a shower. I came back. I put the-- back my one hand only. I have about another $200 or something. And I am ready-- washed and ready. He took me into a cell. There were about 30 Jews, which they caught on the other side. For some reason, half-Jews, Jews which have Polish woman, Christian woman wives already, Christian children. I had the head of the Jewish hospital, on [NON-ENGLISH]. Dr. [? Wortheim ?]-- a very old man-- his two sons, two lawyers, with him-- three. He had a-- a Russian [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a son of a Russian general there-- but mostly Jews-- Jews or half-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: At 6 o'clock, they gave you these two cold potatoes, with a little cabbage, for dinner. I couldn't eat, naturally. In the morning, I had a warm home, a clean bed with that-- with a beautiful towel, with everything. And now, look where I am. How could you eat? I-- I didn't touch. The other boy said, you gonna eat? I'll only have it. You, don't worry. You'll eat. You'll get used to it. But I didn't eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 9 o'clock at night, when-- when they are finished with everything, they-- it's called a appell. He goes from cell to cell to check every cell, how many prisoners are there. He came into our cell with-- and everybody stays; 32 rows of people-- one, the head. He says in Germany, [GERMAN]. We were 30 prisoners, and today we got one more. We are 31, and stays-- and-- and stays and straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Oberste Fuhrer Miller. He has also a whip. And he goes with the whip to my nose, and he said, [GERMAN]? Your name is? Majerczyk Moshe. After the appell, you come to the schriebstube. You come to my office.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went to next. Then the boys said, oh you're going to the office? Leave me your coat, you have such a good coat-- a navy blue coat, a good warm coat. And another guy, leave me your shoes-- why, such good shoes. I had good shoes. They--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was prepared, myself, you know-- I have good-- the best suit, I have; my best coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people assumed that you were gonna die?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I'm gonna never come back-- nobody comes back. So I says, all right, if they will-- if they will kill me, I-- I will be in my shoes. I don't want to leave you the shoes. They are my shoes. I'll let them kill me in my shoes. I went to the schreibstube. You-- you go running. You don't just walk, you know. You-- You run. He's Mr. Miller-- sits in an office, a-- a small office. And the Ukrainian disappears. He brought me there. He disappears.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, you brought a lot of money. Do you want any back of this money? I said, look. In prison, I don't need money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you'll give me-- if-- if you be so kind, you'll give me a little bit food-- some more food, you'll bring me. He takes out a Polish cigarette, a Pulaski, a good cigarette-- Polish. He said, here is a cigarette. I'll bring you some food. But listen, if you are gonna mention this-- what we had now-- talk-- to any of your boyfriends, to any of a prisoner, you are dead, you know. If you want to live, keep your mouth shut, and I'll try my best to bring you something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then why didn't he just keep the money and just not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would he not just keep the money and just--

speaker: interviewee
text: And kill me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- I don't know have the answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, how long were you in this prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait. Wait. I come back. They say, I am back. They all-- the whole prison sees that I am back. Not only I am back, I didn't smoke, and I gave my friend the cigarette. You're not allowed to smoke in prison anyway, but they die for a cigarette. OK. Next day is [NON-ENGLISH]-- next night. It's quiet. [NON-ENGLISH], we had 31 prisoners in the-- he didn't know my name. He didn't. Over the next night, [NON-ENGLISH] with the whip. [NON-ENGLISH] to my nose, you know, with the whip. He touches my nose. [NON-ENGLISH] Majerczyk, and the schreibstube. After the appell, you come to the scheibstube, to the office. Naturally, after the appell, about two hours later, the Ukrainian opens the door, you know, the steel locks, with this steel door, with this-- keys like this. He-- I come to the office. He gives me a newspaper, a Polish kielbasa, a round Polish kielbasa, with a-- with a cigarette again. And he say, here you have it, and go back. I came back to my room. I have a Polish kielbasa. The whole cell started to look at me, which they thought, oh. We have a mole here. Now, you had to do the dirty work every day, to wash the floor in the cell or to-- the-- the-- the bottles to bring out. I didn't-- I didn't do this kind of work. This was not for me.
